content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660        322-5901        322-6441        322-6444

November 30, 1979


William G. Segesta
2180 Milvia Street
Berkeley, CA  94704

Dear Mr. Segesta:

     This is in response to your letter of November 21, 1979, and our telephone conversation of November 30, 1979, in which you request advice concerning your obligations under the Political Reform Act, Government Sections Sections 81000, et seq.1/ The following advice is provided pursuant to Section 83114(b). The facts as I understand them from your letter are as follows.

     You are a member of the Berkeley City Council, which has before it a proposal to enact a rent control ordinance. You have an interest in a duplex in which you live, renting out the second apartment. Your father currently occupies a third unit in this building which does not meet local code requirements for rental units. In addition, you have an interest in another house in Berkeley which contains two rental units, one of which consists of the house proper and the other of which consists of a "third bedroom" addition to the house with a separate entrance and bathroom. The house itself is rented and the addition is occupied by another tenant who is rehabilitating it in lieu of paying rent.

     Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act prohibits public officials from participating in decisions in which they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have "a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally" on various kinds of interests held by the official, including interests in real property worth more than $1,000. By regulation, the Commission has determined that:

_________
1/   All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise stated.

William S. Segesta
November 30, 1979
Page Two


     A material financial effect of a governmental decision
on an official's interests ... is distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally unless the decision
will effect the official's interest in substantially
the same manner as it will affect all members of the
public or a significant segment of the public....

                    2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 13703.

     The Commission has already issued a formal opinion interpreting
this provision in the context of rent control ordinances and has
reached the following conclusion:

     ... In order to be considered a significant segment of
     the public, we think a group usually must be large in
     numbers and heterogeneous in quality. The class of
     persons owning three or fewer units meets both these
     standards and therefore constitutes a significant
     segment of the general public....

                    Opinion requested by
                    John Ferraro, 4 FPPC Ops. 62,67
                    (No. 78-009, Nov. 1, 1978)

     I understand from your letter that you are not sure, given
the facts cited above, how many rental units you own. The second
apartment in your duplex and the house which is rented are clearly
rental units. In addition, the "third bedroom" addition to the
house is also a rental unit as you are receiving something of
value in return for letting the tenant occupy it, i.e., his or
her rehabilitation of the unit. The unit your father is occupying
is not a rental unit, however, as you are not currently renting
it, you have no plans to rent it and it is unclear whether you
would be able to rent it legally even if you wanted to. You
therefore own three or fewer rental units, and the ordinance
under consideration by the Berkeley City Council will affect
your interests in substantially the same manner as they will
affect a significant segment of the public. You therefore do
not have to disqualify yourself from consideration of this ordinance.

     I have enclosed a copy of the Ferraro opinion. If you have
any further questions, please do not hesitate to call me.

                         Sincerely,


                         Sarah Cameron
                         Deputy Chief for
                           Conflicts of Interest
                         Legal Division

SC:plh
Enclosure

City Of Berkeley

CITY COUNCIL

WILLIAM SEGESTA
COUNCIL MEMBER

November 21, 1979

Ted Prim
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California    95804

Dear Mr. Prim,

I am advised that upon request of an elected official, your office will
issue an opinion with respect to a potential conflict of interest situation
faced by that official.

Accordingly, I would like to outline the following situation for the consideration of the commission, pursuant to Government Code 83114(A): I am an
elected member of the Berkeley City Council.

I own, in joint tenancy, an interest in a legal duplex in which I live with
my partner, renting out the second apartment for $ 220.00/month. The ground
floor basement is a third, but illegal, apartment due to its having a six
foot ceiling. I believe the zoning code would permit it as an apartment,
except for the ceiling height. The area is never rented but is used as a
guest room from time to time and is currently housing my father, who pays
no rent, utilities or other household bills. We have no plans to charge
him or anyone else rent for its use.

I also own a 25% interest in a house in Berkeley which I personally am rehabilitating for sale. The house was originally a two-bedroom, single
family building, but many years ago a third "bedroom" was added. It has
its own bathroom and separate entrance, and had been used as a rental unit
by the occupants of the house in past years. It has no kitchen facilities
at this time and we have no plans to add them. The house is rented for
$ 300.00/month and the third bedroom is occupied by a tenant who pays no
rent, but who is engaged in rehabilitating the room in lieu of rent.

Berkeley has passed an interim rent control ordinance and will be considering permanent controls in the next few months. I did not participate in
the vote to pass the interim ordinance because I feared the possibility of
a conflict. I am not certain if I own 3 units as described in Ferraro,
because I do not know if habitable, but illegal, apartments are counted and
I do not know if I am in a special category which could preclude my voting.
My concern about the special category stems from the fact that a major question in the proposed rent control ordinance will revolve around whether to
include or exempt owner-occupied triplexes, duplexes, and four-plexes. In
November, 1978, Berkeley passed an initiative freezing rents but exempting

2180 MILVIA STREET, BERKELEY, CALIFORNIA 94704 • TELEPHONE (415) 644-6359

page 2

owner-occupied buildings of four units or less so currently I am exempted.
The current proposed ordinance does not exempt owner-occupied buildings of
any size.

Additionally, a provision in the proposed rent control ordinances appears
to preclude the eviction of tenants by a new owner of property for occupancy
other than for himself/herself , unless a prior written agreement had been
obtained from the tenants at the onset of their tenancy. For example,
if I own a house which has tenants and sell the house to X, only X can
evict the tenants and move in, in the absence of a written agreement with
the tenants. The only person who can move in is X, not X's children nor
new tenants of X's choosing. This may affect the property in which I hold
a 25% interest, because we expect to sell it and no written agreement has
been obtained.

Thank you for your assistance and if more information is needed, please
contact me at my Council office (415)644-6359, my home (415)527-3847, or
my place of employment (415)948-9320.

Sincerely, [signature]

William G. Segesta

WCS:lr

sections.question: I am an elected member of the Berkeley City Council with interests in rental properties in Berkeley. Berkeley has passed an interim rent control ordinance and will be considering permanent controls in the next few months. I am not certain if I own 3 units as described in Ferraro, because I do not know if habitable, but illegal, apartments are counted and I do not know if I am in a special category which could preclude my voting. My concern about the special category stems from the fact that a major question in the proposed rent control ordinance will revolve around whether to include or exempt owner-occupied triplexes, duplexes, and four-plexes. Additionally, a provision in the proposed rent control ordinances appears to preclude the eviction of tenants by a new owner of property for occupancy other than for himself/herself, unless a prior written agreement had been obtained from the tenants at the onset of their tenancy. This may affect the property in which I hold a 25% interest, because we expect to sell it and no written agreement has been obtained. Should I participate in votes on the proposed rent control ordinance?
sections.question_synthetic: An elected Berkeley City Council member seeks guidance on whether he has a conflict of interest that would preclude him from voting on proposed rent control ordinances, given his ownership interests in rental properties that may be affected differently depending on whether owner-occupied multi-unit buildings are exempted.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. The letter references Government Code 83114(A) and mentions 'Ferraro' (likely a prior FPPC opinion). The document contains a detailed description of the requestor's property interests and the pending ordinances at issue, but does not include a formal QUESTION section or CONCLUSION/advice from FPPC.

Berkeley has passed an interim rent control ordinance and will be considering permanent controls in the next few months. I am not certain if I own 3 units as described in Ferraro, because I do not know if habitable, but illegal, apartments are counted and I do not know if I am in a special category which could preclude my voting. My concern about the special category stems from the fact that a major question in the proposed rent control ordinance will revolve around whether to include or exempt owner-occupied triplexes, duplexes, and four-plexes. Additionally, a provision in the proposed rent control ordinances appears to preclude the eviction of tenants by a new owner of property for occupancy other than for himself/herself, unless a prior written agreement had been obtained from the tenants at the onset of their tenancy. This may affect the property in which I hold a 25% interest, because we expect to sell it and no written agreement has been obtained. Should I participate in votes on the proposed rent control ordinance?

embedding.summary: Incoming FPPC advice request from William G. Segesta, an elected Berkeley City Council member, seeking guidance on potential conflicts of interest regarding his vote on proposed rent control ordinances that may differentially impact his rental property holdings.
